NP
Nancy Pelosi
Speaker of the House (2007–2011, 2019–2023); House Democratic Leader (2003–2023)
LegislatureState/PolicyMacro/Finance
▸
Bottom line
The most consequential modern Speaker, who marshaled the votes for TARP, Dodd-Frank, and the ACA, and presided over the post-COVID fiscal expansion. Assess with HIGH confidence she is the legislative linchpin of both the 2008 crisis response and the 2021–22 stimulus that frames the current fiscal/inflation backdrop. Her family's well-timed equity trades fueled a congressional-trading-reform debate.
Drivers & motivations
Vote-counting mastery; coalition-holding discipline; legacy-through-landmark-legislation.
Worldview & origins
Activist government; markets need crisis backstops and post-crisis regulation.
Decision-making & risk
The era's premier vote-whip; held fractious caucuses together on the hardest votes.
Track record
TARP (2008); Dodd-Frank and the ACA (2010); the 2021 American Rescue Plan and infrastructure/CHIPS-era agenda.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The Pelosi-family stock-trading optics (the STOCK Act / congressional-trading-ban debate centers on her household's tech trades).
Relationships & rivalries
Counterpart to McConnell; the Democratic engine of the crisis and stimulus legislation.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis linchpin of the 2008 and 2021 fiscal responses.
Confidence
HIGH.

The Speaker of the debt-ceiling and shutdown era, whose 2011 brinkmanship produced the sequester and a US credit-rating downgrade. Institutional dealmaking constrained by an ungovernable right flank; pragmatic conservatism.

Fiscal restraint and tax limits, but governance over rupture.
Decision-making & risk
Negotiator caught between deal and caucus; resigned (2015) under Freedom Caucus pressure.
Track record
2011 debt-ceiling standoff (Budget Control Act, sequester, S&P downgrade); 2013 shutdown; failed 'grand bargain.'
Vulnerabilities & levers
The downgrade and shutdowns; loss of caucus control.
Relationships & rivalries
His debt-ceiling fights are direct fiscal-trap exhibits.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis figure for the debt-ceiling stress dynamic.

MODERATE-HIGH.

Supply-side and entitlement-reform conviction; policy-intellectual identity; legacy-through-tax-reform.
Worldview & origins
Pro-growth tax cuts and entitlement restructuring; markets over redistribution.

Policy-driven, deficit-tolerant for tax cuts; the 'reluctant Speaker.'

TCJA (2017); budget blueprints (the 'Ryan budgets'); failed entitlement reform; retired 2019, later on the Fox board.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The TCJA widened deficits without the promised self-financing; entitlement reform stalled.
Relationships & rivalries
His tax cut compounds the fiscal-trap math.

On-thesis driver of the deficit trajectory.

HIGH.

The Speaker whose 15-ballot election and historic mid-term ouster (2023) epitomized the ungovernability of the narrow House majority. Assess with MODERATE confidence his debt-ceiling deal (the Fiscal Responsibility Act) and removal underscore the fragility of the fiscal-governance process.

Power-acquisition ambition; coalition-appeasement; transactional pragmatism.

Majority-management above ideology; deal-making to hold the gavel.

Concession-heavy (the rules giving any member a 'motion to vacate'); ultimately undone by them.
Track record
2023 debt-ceiling deal (Fiscal Responsibility Act); first Speaker ever removed by motion to vacate (Oct 2023).
Vulnerabilities & levers
The concessions that enabled his own removal; governance instability.
Relationships & rivalries
His ouster opened the path to Johnson.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis emblem of fiscal-governance fragility.

MODERATE.

The margin leaves him perpetually one defection from crisis.

On-thesis figure for current fiscal governance.

The rules changes that escalated procedural warfare.
Relationships & rivalries
Counterpart to McConnell; the Senate engine of the crisis-response laws.

Mitch McConnell
Senate Republican Leader (2007–2025)
LegislatureState/PolicyMacro/Finance
▸
Bottom line
The longest-serving Senate party leader, whose mastery of the filibuster, judicial confirmations, and campaign-finance deregulation reshaped American governance. Assess with HIGH confidence his blockade-and-confirm strategy produced the conservative Supreme Court supermajority now dismantling the regulatory state, and his Citizens United-era money-in-politics stance is core to the concentrated-influence thesis.
Drivers & motivations
Institutional-power maximization; judicial-legacy obsession; procedural patience.
Worldview & origins
Minoritarian procedural leverage; deregulated political spending as free speech; courts as the durable prize.
Decision-making & risk
Ruthless long-game tactician; the Garland blockade (2016) and the Barrett rush (2020) bracket his method.
Track record
Blocked Garland, confirmed Gorsuch/Kavanaugh/Barrett (the 6-3 Court); opposed campaign-finance limits for decades; debt-ceiling and shutdown brinkmanship.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The norm-erosion critique; the long-term legitimacy costs of the Court strategy.
Relationships & rivalries
His confirmations created the Roberts/Thomas/Gorsuch/etc. majority the SCOTUS profiles describe.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis architect of the judiciary that constrains financial/tech regulation.
Confidence
HIGH.

PG
Phil Gramm
Chair, Senate Banking Committee (1999–2001)
LegislatureState/PolicyMacro/Finance
▸
Bottom line
The economist-senator who authored the two deregulations most central to this investigation: the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (1999, Glass-Steagall repeal) and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act (2000, the OTC-derivatives exemption). Assess with HIGH confidence he is the single most consequential legislator in dismantling the financial firewalls whose absence defined 2008.
Drivers & motivations
Free-market ideological conviction (PhD economist); deregulatory mission; industry alignment.
Worldview & origins
Markets self-regulate; derivatives and bank-affiliation restrictions are anti-competitive relics.
Decision-making & risk
Aggressive, intellectually assured deregulator; attached the CFMA to an omnibus in the lame-duck session.
Track record
GLBA (with Leach); the CFMA (the 'Enron loophole' and the swaps exemption that buried Born's warning); later UBS vice-chairman.
Vulnerabilities & levers
Both laws are the central capture-thesis exhibits; the UBS role (the bank at the center of later scandals) compounds the optics.
Relationships & rivalries
Legislative counterpart to the Rubin/Greenspan/Summers executive bloc; the UBS tie links to the First Brands/UBS block.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis keystone legislator of financial deregulation.
Confidence
HIGH.

Bottom line
The Senate co-author of Sarbanes-Oxley (2002), the post-Enron corporate-accountability law that created the PCAOB and reshaped audit and disclosure. Drivers & motivations
Investor-protection conviction; institutional integrity; reform-in-crisis resolve.
Worldview & origins
Robust auditing and executive accountability are prerequisites for market trust.
Decision-making & risk
Deliberate, principled; seized the post-Enron/WorldCom moment for structural reform.
Track record
Sarbanes-Oxley Act (2002): PCAOB, auditor-independence rules, CEO/CFO certification, whistleblower protections.
Vulnerabilities & levers
Compliance-cost criticism; SOX did not prevent 2008 (a different failure mode).
Relationships & rivalries
Legislative counterweight to the deregulation Gramm advanced.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis pillar of disclosure-integrity law.

HIGH.

RS
Richard Shelby
Chair, Senate Banking Committee (2003–2007, 2015–2017)
LegislatureState/PolicyMacro/Finance
▸
Bottom line
The long-serving Banking chair who presided before the crisis and later drove the 2018 partial rollback of Dodd-Frank (S.2155). Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his deregulatory tailoring raised the systemic-bank threshold in ways implicated by the 2023 regional-bank failures.
Drivers & motivations
Deregulatory, bank-friendly conservatism; Alabama financial-industry constituency; institutional seniority.
Worldview & origins
Post-crisis rules overburden regional banks; tailoring restores competitiveness.
Decision-making & risk
Seniority-leveraging; pushed threshold relief.
Track record
Pre-crisis Banking chair; the Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief and Consumer Protection Act (2018, S.2155) raising the SIFI threshold from $50B to $250B — later cited in the SVB (~$210B) failure debate.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The 2018 rollback's link to the 2023 mid-size-bank stress.
Relationships & rivalries
Deregulatory counterpoint to Dodd/Brown; the tailoring tied to the FDIC/HTM blocks.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis figure for the threshold-rollback and 2023 failures.
Confidence
MODERATE-HIGH.

CD
Chris Dodd
Chair, Senate Banking Committee (2007–2011)
LegislatureState/PolicyMacro/Finance
▸
Bottom line
The Senate namesake of the Dodd-Frank Act (2010), the most sweeping financial-regulatory overhaul since the New Deal. Assess with HIGH confidence Dodd-Frank — the CFPB, the Volcker Rule, FSOC, orderly-liquidation, derivatives clearing — is the defining legislative response to 2008 and the framework the current deregulators are unwinding.
Drivers & motivations
Crisis-reform mandate; legacy-through-landmark-law; coalition-brokering.
Worldview & origins
Systemic risk requires comprehensive regulation, consumer protection, and resolution authority.
Decision-making & risk
Negotiation-heavy compromise-builder; shepherded a vast bill through a narrow window.
Track record
Dodd-Frank (2010); the AIG-bonus and 'Countrywide VIP loan' controversies dogged him; did not seek re-election; later MPAA chair.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The Countrywide-loan optics; Dodd-Frank's complexity and partial rollback.
Relationships & rivalries
House counterpart Barney Frank; the framework the Crapo/Shelby/2025 deregulators trimmed.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis keystone of post-crisis financial regulation.
Confidence
HIGH.

Counterweight to Scott/Crapo; aligned with Warren on bank accountability.

The House co-author of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (1999) that repealed Glass-Steagall. Assess with HIGH confidence GLBA is the structural deregulation that permitted the financial-conglomerate model at the center of the 2008 architecture — though Leach, a moderate, later voiced reservations.
Drivers & motivations
Moderate-Republican modernization view; belief that the Depression-era walls were outdated.
Worldview & origins
Financial modernization improves competitiveness; affiliation restrictions are obsolete.
Decision-making & risk
Bipartisan, intellectual; the moderate face of a deregulation he later partly regretted.
Track record
Gramm-Leach-Bliley (1999); later expressed concern about the conglomerate risks GLBA enabled; chaired the NEH.
Vulnerabilities & levers
GLBA's role in enabling the too-big-to-fail conglomerates.
Relationships & rivalries
House counterpart to Gramm; the moderate co-author.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis structural-deregulation legislator.

HIGH.

LegislatureState/PolicyMacro/Finance

Bottom line
The House namesake of Dodd-Frank and the chamber's sharpest financial-policy mind during the crisis. Assess with HIGH confidence he is the legislative architect, with Dodd, of the post-2008 regulatory framework — and his later board seat at Signature Bank (which failed in 2023) is an ironic capstone.
Drivers & motivations
Activist-government conviction; intellectual combativeness; consumer-and-systemic-protection focus.
Worldview & origins
Markets need strong regulation and consumer protection; systemic risk must be contained.
Decision-making & risk
Quick-witted, hands-on legislator; drove the bill's House passage.
Track record
Dodd-Frank (2010); the CFPB and Volcker Rule; later joined Signature Bank's board — the crypto-exposed bank seized in March 2023.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The Signature board seat (and his post-passage softening on a key threshold) is a pointed irony given the 2023 failures.
Relationships & rivalries
Senate counterpart Chris Dodd; the Signature tie links him to the 2023-bank-failure block.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis architect of post-crisis regulation, with an ironic 2023 coda.
Confidence
HIGH.

Institutional-survival conviction (saving the euro); technocratic mastery; credibility-as-tool.
Worldview & origins
Central-bank credibility can substitute for fiscal action; the currency union must be defended at any cost.

Calculated, minimalist signaling backed by unlimited capacity (OMT was never used - the threat sufficed).
Track record
'Whatever it takes' + OMT; negative rates and QE; later Italy PM and author of the 2024 EU-competitiveness report warning Europe is falling behind on tech/AI.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The QE-forever critique; Europe's structural stagnation his report diagnosed.
Relationships & rivalries
ECB lineage to Lagarde; counterpart to the Fed's post-2012 regime.
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis model of credibility-driven monetary backstops.

HIGH.

George Soros

Bottom line
The macro-trader whose theory of REFLEXIVITY - that perceptions and prices feed back on each other to create self-reinforcing booms and busts - is a direct intellectual frame for the project's analysis of the AI/credit reflexive loop. Assess with MODERATE confidence reflexivity is the most apt market-theory lens for the self-marking circular core.
Drivers & motivations
Reflexivity conviction; open-society ideology; macro-trading instinct.
Worldview & origins
Markets are not efficient; participants' biased perceptions shape the fundamentals they then react to (reflexivity), producing bubbles and crashes.
Decision-making & risk
Aggressive macro bets on reflexive turning points (the 1992 sterling break); fallibility-aware.
Track record
'Broke the Bank of England' (1992); the Quantum Fund record; 'The Alchemy of Finance' (reflexivity); Open Society philanthropy (a frequent target of conspiracy narratives - noted and excluded from claims here).
Vulnerabilities & levers
Politicized target of disinformation; reflexivity is a frame, not a timing tool.
Relationships & rivalries
Intellectual frame for the project's reflexive-loop thesis (self-marked value, M1-M4 reflexive marks).
Outlook & indicators
On-thesis conceptual lens (reflexivity) for the bubble dynamics.
Confidence
MODERATE.
Grading

LS
Lawrence Summers
Treasury Secretary (1999–2001); NEC Director (2009–2010); Harvard President (2001–2006)
RegulatorMacro/FinanceState/Policy
▸
Bottom line
A central architect of late-1990s financial deregulation who later became the loudest establishment Cassandra on 2021 inflation. Assess with HIGH confidence his fingerprints on Gramm-Leach-Bliley (Glass-Steagall repeal) and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act (which exempted OTC derivatives), and MODERATE-HIGH that his 2021 'too much fiscal stimulus' warning was directionally correct against the Fed/Treasury consensus.
Drivers & motivations
Intellectual dominance; being proven right; proximity to power across administrations.
Worldview & origins
Neoliberal-Keynesian hybrid: pro-market deregulation in the 1990s, but a believer in large counter-cyclical intervention and, latterly, in supply-constrained inflation risk and 'secular stagnation.'
Decision-making & risk
Combative, supremely self-assured, willing to break with consensus publicly; prone to abrasive certainty.
Track record
Backed OTC-derivatives exemption (CFMA 2000) and Glass-Steagall repeal (1999); opposed Brooksley Born's CFTC swaps-oversight push; warned in 2021 that ARP-scale stimulus would overheat the economy (vindicated); long associated with the 'activist Treasury issuance' critique he now partly voices.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The Born clash and CFMA are cited in post-2008 and crypto-derivatives post-mortems; the Harvard presidency ended amid controversy; perceived as emblematic of the bipartisan dereg consensus he now critiques.
Relationships & rivalries
Tied to Rubin (mentor) and Geithner (protégé); public sparring partner of the Yellen/Powell 'transitory' camp; informal advisor across Democratic administrations.
Outlook & indicators
His dereg legacy and his inflation warnings are both load-bearing in the macro narrative; a credibility weather-vane for elite consensus.
Confidence
HIGH (legislative record); MODERATE-HIGH (intent and influence interpreted).
Grading
fact + labeled interpretation

Founder-CEO of CCDH and a former UK Labour adviser who ran the NGO from Washington from 2021 - celebrated as a platform-accountability campaigner and attacked as a 'censorship-by-proxy' operator. Jim Jordan subpoenaed CCDH (2023) over its funders and its communications with platforms and federal agencies, which CCDH resisted. Funding transparency is the live contested question.
Drivers & motivations
Platform accountability; reducing online harms; influence over moderation/advertiser policy.
Worldview & origins
Harm-reduction campaigner; argues platforms externalize societal damage and must be pressured.
Decision-making & risk
Media-active, research-driven, litigation-tested; promotes the STAR regulatory framework.
Track record
Founded CCDH (UK, 2017-18; co-founded with Labour's Morgan McSweeney) and built its influential 'Disinformation Dozen' and platform-harm research; moved operations to Washington in 2021, reportedly working closely with the Biden administration; survived Musk's X lawsuit (dismissed 2024).
Vulnerabilities & levers
Funding opacity - 2021 tax records show ~$1.47m raised, ~75% via the Schwab Charitable donor-advised fund (a pass-through critics call 'dark money'); the Jim Jordan subpoena over funders and government/platform communications; partisan-source allegations (e.g., of IRS-filing misstatements, or of coordination with foreign governments) that are contested and not independently established.
Relationships & rivalries
Co-founded CCDH with McSweeney (Starmer's chief of staff) - a Labour-to-content-moderation link; adversary of Musk/X; reported Biden-administration engagement; central node in the content-moderation-NGO thread.
Outlook & indicators
A focal figure in the deplatforming-vs-free-speech contest the project tracks.

MM
Morgan McSweeney
Downing Street Chief of Staff to PM Keir Starmer
State/PolicyExecutive
▸
Bottom line
Starmer's chief strategist and Downing Street chief of staff - architect of Labour's 2024 election machine - who also co-founded the Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH) in the UK (2018) and ran Labour Together during the period it was fined by the Electoral Commission for failing to declare £700k+ in donations. Assess with HIGH confidence on the roles and the fine; an alleged cover-up is contested and the watchdog found no evidence of offenses by him.
Drivers & motivations
Labour electoral and governing success; message discipline; control of No.10.
Worldview & origins
Pragmatic centrist operator; focused on power-consolidation and delivery.
Decision-making & risk
Strategy-driven, control-oriented, low public profile by design.
Track record
Ran Labour Together (2017-2020) - which the Electoral Commission fined ~£14,250 for undeclared donations exceeding £700k - then built the 2024 campaign and became No.10 chief of staff steering personnel, message, and the digital-ID/growth agendas; reported co-founder of CCDH (2018).
Vulnerabilities & levers
The Labour Together donor-reporting fine; Conservative claims (from leaked emails) of an attempted cover-up framed as an admin error (contested; the Electoral Commission found no evidence of offenses and declined to reopen); the CCDH co-founding ties him to the contested content-moderation nexus.
Relationships & rivalries
Gatekeeper to Starmer; co-founded CCDH with Imran Ahmed (linking the Labour, donor, and content-moderation strands); intersects the donor and digital-ID maps.
Outlook & indicators
A node controlling access and strategy in the Starmer government.
Confidence
MODERATE-HIGH (role documented).
Grading
fact (roles, fine, CCDH co-founding) + contested (cover-up allegation)

PM
Peter Mandelson
Former UK Ambassador to the United States (2025, sacked); Labour grandee; ex-EU Trade Commissioner; founder, Global Counsel
State/PolicyGeopoliticsCapital
▸
Bottom line
A New Labour architect and consummate networker appointed UK ambassador to Washington in 2025 and SACKED months later (11 Sep 2025) after the US House Oversight Committee's release of Jeffrey Epstein's 'birthday book' and emails exposed a far deeper Epstein friendship than disclosed - including a letter calling Epstein his 'best pal' and messages of support after Epstein's 2008 conviction. Assess with HIGH confidence on the sacking and the Epstein correspondence; combined with his Global Counsel commercial ties (Russia/China/Gulf), he is the roster's clearest case of conflict-and-association scrutiny.
Drivers & motivations
Influence at the center of UK/EU/US power; commercial advisory; transatlantic relations.
Worldview & origins
Pro-market New Labour internationalist; comfortable bridging politics and business.
Decision-making & risk
Networked, strategic, controversy-weathering (multiple past resignations).
Track record
Architected New Labour messaging; twice resigned from Blair's cabinet; EU Trade Commissioner; founded the advisory firm Global Counsel (clients across sectors and states); appointed UK ambassador to the US (2025) and dismissed within months when released Epstein emails showed the 'depth and extent' of the friendship was 'materially different' from what was known at appointment.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The Epstein correspondence and sacking (documented via House Oversight + UK government); the Global Counsel client list and past associations with controversial financiers and foreign actors feed sustained conflict/foreign-ties scrutiny; a history of forced resignations.
Relationships & rivalries
Blair-era ally; bridges UK politics, the EU, the US, and global commercial interests; the Epstein link connects him to the Epstein-document thread; Starmer appointed (then sacked) him.
Outlook & indicators
A node connecting UK influence, transatlantic diplomacy, and commercial advisory.
Confidence
MODERATE-HIGH (career documented); specific conflict claims graded case-by-case.
Grading
fact (sacking + Epstein emails documented) + labeled interpretation (commercial-conflict scrutiny)

Blair-continuity and past-engagement scrutiny; the China-embassy/intel-sharing tensions land on his desk.
Relationships & rivalries
Blair-Starmer continuity node; tied to the UK-comms-security and allied-intel threads.

A node at the apex of UK security decision-making.

MODERATE-HIGH (role documented).

CEO of the WFA, the global advertiser body that ran GARM, the brand-safety initiative X and Rumble alleged was an illegal ad boycott and which the WFA shut down in 2024 amid litigation and a congressional probe. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his role over the advertiser-alliance thread.

Worldview & origins
Industry-coordination advocate; framed GARM as voluntary brand-safety, not a boycott.
Decision-making & risk
Consensus-building, industry-facing, litigation-cautious.
Track record
Led the WFA's GARM initiative (2019-2024); discontinued GARM in Aug 2024 after X's antitrust suit, citing resource drain, amid the House Judiciary probe.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The 'illegal boycott' allegation (unresolved on merits); the deplatforming-vs-brand-safety dispute.
Relationships & rivalries
Central to the GARM/advertiser-alliance strand of the censorship thread.
Outlook & indicators
A node in the advertiser-coordination deplatforming debate.
Confidence
MODERATE-HIGH (role documented); boycott allegation contested.

fact + labeled interpretation

The Labour cabinet minister who, as Local Government Secretary, approved China's Royal Mint Court mega-embassy in January 2026 over national-security objections — the decisionmaker of record on that comms-infrastructure exposure. Delivering government planning/housing priorities; executing the approval decision.

Labour delivery minister; balanced diplomatic-property obligations against security objections.

Process-driven, decision-of-record on the embassy planning call.
Track record
As the responsible secretary, granted the Royal Mint Court embassy approval (Jan 2026) after MI5/MI6 clearance despite MI5/GCHQ residual-risk warnings and US objections.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The security-risk and US-objection optics of the embassy approval attach to his decision.
Relationships & rivalries
Decisionmaker in the Chinese-embassy/UK-comms strand of the influence map.
Outlook & indicators
A node of record on the embassy-approval decision.
Confidence
MODERATE (role + decision documented).

fact + labeled interpretation

Bottom line
Founder-CEO of Polymarket, the dominant crypto prediction market, which moved from a 2022 CFTC settlement to mainstream prominence and a US re-entry amid the friendlier 2025-26 regime. Assess with HIGH confidence on his role at the center of the prediction-markets thread.

Prediction-market scale and legitimacy; event-derivatives as information infrastructure; US market access.
Worldview & origins
Crypto-native builder; frames prediction markets as truth-seeking information tools.
Decision-making & risk
Fast-moving, regulation-navigating, media-prominent.
Track record
Built Polymarket to record election-cycle volumes; settled with the CFTC (2022) over unregistered swaps; pursued US re-entry (ICE/Intercontinental Exchange investment context).
Vulnerabilities & levers
Regulatory-status questions; manipulation/observation-effect critiques of prediction markets.
Relationships & rivalries
Tied to the ICE/Trump-family/a16z prediction-market cap-table examined in the block.
Outlook & indicators
Central figure in the prediction-markets thread.
Confidence
HIGH (role + CFTC record).

fact + labeled interpretation

Operator caught between US national-security demands and Chinese ownership.

Led TikTok through the US divestiture law, the Project Texas data plan, and the on-again/off-again ban/deal; a focal point of the China-tech and data-security threads.
Vulnerabilities & levers
The China-ownership control question; data-security distrust; political football status.
Relationships & rivalries
Ties to the China-tech and influence threads; counterpart to US regulators (Carr/FTC).
Outlook & indicators
A node in the US-China tech-decoupling and data-security map.

HIGH (role documented).
